author: @grendel1960a
text: what goes around comes around, about 8 years (maybe more now) ago I purchased a davinci Ai0 3d printer, it wasnt cheap, but at that time it was the only 3d printer that came ready built, ready to go out of the box, and it doesnt look a lot different than your latest printers. there are a few minor differences, it has a glass bed, its an awful lot slower, but it plays well with ABS, which is what i mainly use it for (well ABS and TPU) the other issue is that from the start it was tied to the davinci filament, each spool came in a cartridge and the temperature and print speed was set by a chip in the cartridge. Since then I have purchased a cartridge resetter, where you can reset the chip for a new filament or temperatures and reset it back to 0m count (as if the chip got to 240m it ceased to work.) yjr machine still gets regular outings when I want an ABS part, but the majority of the work nowadays is done on my flsun. I didnt want to get into the mechanics of setting up a 3d printer, I just wanted it to work, over the years I have built a library of over 1000 different stl files for printing.

author: @SimonPlatten
text:
I have had 7 3D printers including the latest:
	Bambu P1S (Favourite and so much better than all the others I've owned)
	QIDI PLUS4 3D Printer, Max 600mm/s High-Speed Printing
	Creality K1 Max 3D Printer Official, 600mm/s Max
	ANYCUBIC Kobra Max 3D Printer, Large 3D Printer
	Voxelab Aquila D1 FDM 3D Printer
	Voxelab Aquila Pro 3D Printer Auto Leveling,DIY FDM 3D Printer Kit
	QIDI TECHNOLOGY Dual Extruder Desktop 3D Printer
Looking at the build of the Bambu P1S and comparing it with the QIDI PLUS4, I’m sure that the parts are designed by the same company as they are almost identical.  The Bambu has been consistently reliable and so far "touch wood" its been great!  The others didn't fair to well.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that is a lot of printers mate and a good range and I think I agree with your conclusion lol - a bit like cars really

author: @MadeByGrumpy
text:
Any idea if it will get any form of AMS? They can make it very competitive on price but without an AMS it wouldn’t interest me. 
I have a Bambu A1, A1 Mini, Sovol SV03 and an Elegoo Neptune 3 Plus. What I really want is a larger format core XY with an AMS, to replace the SV03 and Neptune 3 Plus.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that's funny mate I had the AMS on the X1C and took it off because i found it unnecessary - actually a bit of a trial - but if that is the deal breaker for you - I believe they are planning one

replies.author: @MadeByGrumpy
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering That's good then - I would  have been surprised if they weren't going to release a printer in this class without an AMS.
I know most of your prints are engineering related, and so do not require multi-coloured filaments, but have you tried using a specialist support filament to make removing the supports easier? This is a really useful use case for an AMS for some highly detailed prints.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: @MadeByGrumpy you are right mate most of my prints are technical and I think you are also right it does lead to process differences - i tried support prints but again i didn't find much cause to keep on using them - but work flows are always the same - dependent on what you are doing - so I can see your needs are a fair bit different from mine - thanks for pointing that out
